750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by

1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}

1172	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1173	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1174	
1175	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1176	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1177	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1178	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1179	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1180	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1181	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1182	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1183	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1184	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1185	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1186	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1187	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1188	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1189	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1190	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1191	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1192	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1193	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1194	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1195	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1265	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),

1267	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1268	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1269	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1270	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1271	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1272	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1273	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1274	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1275	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1276	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1277	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1278	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1279	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1280	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1281	
1282	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1283	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1284	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1285	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1286	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1287	\end{definition}

1289	\begin{definition}
1290	
1291	[Section {\v C}ech complex of a presheaf of modules]
1292	  \label{def:section_cech_complex}
1293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplex,
1294	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicial}
1295	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
1296	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves,
1297	  % definition of $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
1298	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
1299	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
1300	  % $$
1301	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1302	  % =
1303	  % \prod\nolimits_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p + 1}}
1304	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1305	  % $$
1306	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
1307	  % $$
1308	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1309	  % \longrightarrow
1310	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1311	  % $$
1312	  % by the formula
1313	  % $$
1314	  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
1315	  % =
1316	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^{p + 1}
1317	  % (-1)^j
1318	  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1319	  % $$
1320	  % It is straightforward to see that $d \circ d = 0$."
1321	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1322	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1323	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1324	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1325	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1326	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1327	  \[
1328	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1329	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1330	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1331	  \]
1332	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1333	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1334	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1335	  \[
1336	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1337	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1338	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1339	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1340	  \]
1341	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1342	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1343	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1344	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1345	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1346	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1347	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1348	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1349	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1350	\end{definition}

1395	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We saw in the discussion just above the lemma that
1396	% $$
1397	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(
1398	% (j_{i_0\ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_p}},
1399	% \mathcal{F})
1400	% =
1401	% \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1402	% $$
1403	% Hence we see that it is indeed the case that the direct sum
1404	% $$
1405	% \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
1406	% (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
1407	% $$
1408	% represents the functor
1409	% $$
1410	% \mathcal{F}
1411	% \longmapsto
1412	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1413	% $$
1414	% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}
1415	% we see that there is a complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ with terms
1416	% as given. It is a simple matter to see that the maps are as given

1418	\begin{proof}
1419	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1420	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1421	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1422	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1423	  \[
1424	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1425	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1426	    \bigr)
1427	    \;\cong\;
1428	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1429	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1430	    \;\cong\;
1431	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1432	  \]
1433	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1434	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1435	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1436	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1437	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1438	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1439	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1440	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1441	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1442	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1443	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1444	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1445	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1446	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1447	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1448	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1449	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1450	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1451	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1452	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1453	\end{proof}

1479	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1480	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :

1489	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term

1496	\begin{lemma}
1497	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1498	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1500	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1501	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1502	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1503	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1504	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1505	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1506	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1507	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1508	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1509	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1510	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1511	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1512	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1513	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1514	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1515	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1516	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1517	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1518	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1519	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1520	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1521	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1522	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1523	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1524	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1525	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1526	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1527	\end{lemma}
1528	
1529	\begin{proof}
1530	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1531	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1532	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1533	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1534	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1535	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1536	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1537	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1538	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1539	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1540	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1541	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1542	  quasi-isomorphism.
1543	\end{proof}

1545	\begin{lemma}
1546	
1547	\leanok
1548	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]

1551	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,

1554	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1555	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1556	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1557	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1558	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1559	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1560	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1561	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1562	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1563	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1564	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1565	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1566	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1567	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1568	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1569	  \[
1570	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1571	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1572	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1573	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1574	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1575	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1576	  \]
1577	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1578	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1579	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1580	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1581	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1582	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1583	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1584	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1585	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1586	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1587	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1588	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1589	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1590	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1591	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1592	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by
1593	  \(\operatorname{rfl}\)) composed with
1594	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\) at \(V\). The full degreewise
1595	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)
1596	  (evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the coproduct, giving
1597	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_\sigma
1598	  (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\)),
1599	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\) (a surviving summand with
1600	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\)), and
1601	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\) (a summand with
1602	  \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) is the zero object); collapsing the coproduct over the
1603	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1604	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1605	\end{lemma}

1607	\begin{proof}
1608	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1609	  \leanok
1610	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1611	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1612	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1613	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1614	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1615	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1616	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1617	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1618	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1619	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1620	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1621	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1622	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1623	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1624	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1625	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.

1654	  % $$

1657	  % $$
1658	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1659	  % $$

1667	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1668	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1669	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1670	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1671	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1672	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1673	  \[
1674	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}

1678	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1679	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),

1681	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1682	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1683	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1684	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1685	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are

2073	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
2074	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}

2076	  \mathlibok
2077	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib

2079	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2080	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2081	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.
2082	\end{lemma}

2090	  For the identity ring map \(\alpha = \mathbf{1}\) over a scheme \(X\),

2268	
2269	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2270	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2271	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2272	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2273	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2274	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By

2276	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2277	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2278	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2279	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).

2282	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of

2288	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the

2290	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of

2294	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2295	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).

2298	  opposite-transport bridge. The augmentation \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}
2299	  \mathcal{U}\) is a quasi-isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso});
2300	  taking its opposite via the \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.op}\) machinery and
2301	  applying \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{I}\) keeps it a
2302	  quasi-isomorphism, since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules
2303	  (Part 1) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2304	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\),
2305	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2306	  mapped-opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isos
2307	  \(\operatorname{homCechComplexMapOpIso}\)

2309	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2310	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2311	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2312	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the mapped image of the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target

2465	  % Assume there exists a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$

2478	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
2479	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.}
2480	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, let
2481	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and suppose there is a set
2482	  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of open coverings such that: (1) every \(\mathcal{U} :
2483	  U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) has \(U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}\) and
2484	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\); (2) for each
2485	  \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(U\) occurring in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
2486	  cofinal among all open coverings of \(U\); and (3) for every \(\mathcal{U} \in
2487	  \mathrm{Cov}\) the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
2488	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). Then
2489	  \[
2490	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0 \text{ and any }
2491	      U \in \mathcal{B}.
2492	  \]
2493	  This is the basis-comparison vehicle that upgrades the standard-cover {\v C}ech
2494	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} to the Serre vanishing of
2495	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}: instantiating \(\mathcal{B}\) as the affine
2496	  opens and \(\mathrm{Cov}\) as the standard affine covers, condition (3) is exactly
2497	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. The formal target of this lemma is the
2498	  \emph{vanishing} conclusion \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\) under
2499	  hypotheses (1)--(3), not an explicit isomorphism \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
2500	  \mathcal{F}) \cong H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of {\v C}ech and sheaf cohomology; only
2501	  the positive-degree vanishing is needed downstream, and it is all the statement
2502	  establishes.
2503	\end{lemma}

2622	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
2623	
2624	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
2625	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
2626	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
2627	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
2628	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
2629	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.
2630	
2631	\begin{lemma}
2632	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
2633	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
2634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
2635	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
2636	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
2637	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
2638	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
2639	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
2640	  % \begin{equation}
2641	  % \label{equation-extended}
2642	  % 0 \to
2643	  % M \to
2644	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
2645	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
2646	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
2647	  % \ldots
2648	  % \end{equation}
2649	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
2650	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
2651	  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
2652	  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
2653	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
2654	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
2655	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
2656	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
2657	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
2658	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
2659	  \[
2660	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
2661	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
2662	    \qquad
2663	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
2664	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
2665	  \]
2666	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
2667	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
2668	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
2669	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
2670	\end{lemma}
2671	
2672	\begin{proof}
2673	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
2674	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
2675	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
2676	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
2677	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
2678	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
2679	  localisations
2680	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
2681	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
2682	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
2683	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
2684	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
2685	\end{proof}

2687	\begin{lemma}
2688	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
2689	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
2690	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
2691	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
2692	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
2693	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
2694	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
2695	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
2696	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
2697	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
2698	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
2699	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
2700	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
2701	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
2702	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
2703	  \[
2704	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
2705	      \;\cong\;
2706	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
2707	  \]
2708	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
2709	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
2710	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
2711	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
2712	\end{lemma}
2713	
2714	\begin{proof}
2715	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
2716	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
2717	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
2718	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
2719	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
2720	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
2721	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
2722	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
2723	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
2724	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
2725	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
2726	  additive homology-preserving functor.
2727	\end{proof}

2729	\begin{lemma}
2730	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
2731	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
2732	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_iso_sheafify_presheafHomology,
2733	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardResolutionPresheafComplex}

2735	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
2736	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
2737	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
2738	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
2739	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
2740	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.

2742	  % associated to the presheaves
2743	  % $$
2744	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
2745	  % $$
2746	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
2747	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
2748	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."

2750	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
2751	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
2752	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
2753	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
2754	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
2755	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
2756	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
2757	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
2758	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
2759	  \[
2760	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
2761	      \;\cong\;
2762	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
2763	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
2764	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
2765	  \]
2766	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
2767	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
2768	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream
2769	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} and
2770	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
2771	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
2772	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
2773	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
2774	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
2775	
2776	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
2777	  of the preimage,
2778	  \[
2779	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
2780	      \;=\;
2781	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
2782	  \]
2783	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
2784	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the
2785	  resolution-internal sheafify-of-objectwise-homology form.
2786	\end{lemma}

2788	\begin{proof}
2789	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
2790	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
2791	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
2792	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
2793	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
2794	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
2795	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
2796	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
2797	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
2798	  \[
2799	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
2800	      \;\cong\;
2801	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
2802	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
2803	  \]
2804	  which is the stated isomorphism. Since the sheafification of a presheaf vanishes
2805	  exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens, this yields the
2806	  affine-local vanishing criterion.
2807	\end{proof}

2832	      \(k \geq 0\),

2852	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
2853	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
2854	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
2855	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
2856	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
2857	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
2858	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the

2859	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
2860	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).

2862	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
2863	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
2864	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
2865	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
2866	
2867	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
2868	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
2869	  sheafification of

2877	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
2878	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine

2881	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).

2914	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
2915	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
2916	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
2917	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =

2919	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
2920	  \[
2921	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
2922	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
2923	  \]

2927	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
2928	% the sheaf
2929	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
2930	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
2931	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
2932	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
2933	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
2934	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
2935	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
2936	% opens we win."

2939	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
2940	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
2941	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
2942	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
2943	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
2944	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
2945	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
2946	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
2947	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
2948	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
2949	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
2950	
2951	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
2952	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
2953	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
2954	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
2955	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
2956	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
2957	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
2958	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
2959	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
2960	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
2961	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
2962	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
2963	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
2964	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
2965	  \(k \geq 1\).

3054	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
3055	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
3056	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
3057	  resolution,
3058	  \[
3059	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
3060	      \;\cong\;
3061	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
3062	  \]
3063	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
3064	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
3065	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
3066	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
3067	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
3068	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
3069	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
3070	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
3071	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
3072	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
3073	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
3074	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
3075	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
3076	\end{proof}
3077

---

## Summary verdict

**HARD GATE CLEARS — no must-fix findings.**

Both `FreePresheafComplex.lean` and `CechAcyclic.lean` may proceed to prover dispatch this iter.

All three blueprint chapters are `complete: true` and `correct: true`.

---

## Directive-specific confirmations (5 items)

### Item 1 — No `CombinatorialCech.*` names in free-eval proof sketches

**RESOLVED.**

Searched all proof sketches under `lem:cech_free_eval_*`. The NOTE block at lines 1628–1631 explicitly
documents the design decision:

> "FreeCechEngine replicates the API of the private CombinatorialCech namespace, visible only to
> CechAcyclic.lean. This is a deliberate copy to cross the module-privacy barrier."

All proof sketches reference `FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy`, `FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy_spec`,
`FreeCechEngine.combDifferential`, etc. No `CombinatorialCech.*` name appears in any free-eval block.

### Item 2 — `FreeCechEngine` namespace blueprinted (`lem:free_cech_engine`)

**RESOLVED.**

`lem:free_cech_engine` block is present at lines 1632–1697. It lists:

```
\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combDifferential,
    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combSign,
    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy,
    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy_spec,
    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.cons_comp_succAbove_succ,
    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combSign_flip}
```

Statement prose characterises the namespace as the public constant-coefficient combinatorial Čech
homotopy engine. SOURCE: comment points to `references/stacks-cohomology.tex` (L1245–1268).

### Item 3 — `lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso` with accurate `\uses{}` and real proof sketch

**RESOLVED.**

Block present at lines 1699–1775. Key fields:

```
\label{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}
\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEvalEngineIso}
\uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
```

The `\uses{}` is accurate and minimal:
- `lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise` supplies the degreewise `cechFreeEval_X` identification;
- `lem:free_cech_engine` supplies `FreeCechEngine.combDifferential` for the differential match.

The proof sketch is substantive:
- **Degreewise identification**: applies `cechFreeEval_X` degree-by-degree via `ChainComplex.ext`;
  each degree-`p` component is `⊕_{σ : Fin(p+1)→I₁} O_X(V)`, matching the evaluated free complex and
  the engine complex by `Limits.Sigma.hom_ext` + `Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso`.
- **Differential match**: shows `FreeCechEngine.combDifferential` coincides with the differential
  induced by `PresheafOfModules.evaluation V`, term by term via sigma-decomposition.

This node unblocks the `cechFreeComplex_quasiIso` route: the 6-link `\uses` chain at
`lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso` (line 1969) now includes `lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso`.

### Item 4 — `lem:section_cech_homology_exact` uses `ShortComplex.ab_exact_iff`, 3-sub-lemma chain

**RESOLVED.**

Block at lines 605–747. Lines 680–688 give the explicit rationale:

> "The degree-p object of `sectionCechComplex R s F` is the categorical product `∏_{σ : Fin(p+1)→I} F(D(sσ))`
> in the category **Ab** of abelian groups, not an R-module product. Therefore
> `ShortComplex.moduleCat_exact_iff` does NOT apply. The correct reduction uses
> `ShortComplex.ab_exact_iff` (Lemma \ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact})."

The three sub-lemmas are present with explicit intended Lean names:

| Block | `\lean{}` name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `lem:section_cech_product_equiv` | `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv` | degreewise ≅ with module-level product |
| `lem:section_cech_coface_match` | `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCofaceMatch` | coface differentials match under the equiv |
| `lem:section_cech_ab_exact` | `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAbExact` | ShortComplex.ab_exact_iff closes exactness |

The parent block `\uses{}` includes all three sub-lemmas plus `def:qcoh_sections_localized` and
`def:section_cech_complex`.

### Item 5 — `def:qcoh_sections_localized` lists `basicOpen_sprod` + `qcohRestriction_eq_comparison`

**RESOLVED.**

Block at lines 528–603. `\lean{}` list (lines 535–545):

```
\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison}
```

Prose at lines 565–585 explains their roles:
- `basicOpen_sprod`: the multi-index intersection identity `⋂ D(s_{σ(k)}) = D(∏ s_{σ(k)})`;
- `qcohRestriction_eq_comparison`: identifies the restriction map `F(D(g₁)) → F(D(g₁g₂))` with the
  away-localization comparison `M_{g₁} → M_{g₁g₂}`.
